meta.title: Drift — Nomad Super App
meta.subtitle: A design concept exploring what happens when city discovery, remote jobs, and IRL community are organised around one place — not three separate platforms.

data.headline: A city isn't a location. It's a decision.
data.body: When a digital nomad considers relocating, they're not asking 'where is Prague?' They're asking three questions simultaneously: Can I afford it? Is there work I can actually get paid for? Will there be people worth knowing when I get there? Right now, those three questions live on three different platforms that don't talk to each other. This concept explores what the design looks like when you structure the answer around the city — not the task — and what hard decisions that forces you to make about information hierarchy, social proof, and what a 'profile' for a place actually needs to contain.

data.label: worldwide in 2023 — up 131% since 2019
data.body: Source: MBO Partners State of Independence 2023. A generation of workers who move between cities has already arrived. The tooling to support how they actually make relocation decisions hasn't caught up — and the gap between nomad population growth and the product infrastructure built for them is exactly where Drift positions itself.

before.label: How nomads research a city today
items: Nomad List — check livability score and cost of living
Remote job board (Remote.co, We Work Remotely) — scan for open roles
Meetup or Telegram group — find local nomad events and community
Twitter or Discord — ask if anyone's been there, what it's actually like
Google Maps — manually check cafés, coworking spaces, neighborhood feel

after.label: What Drift proposes
items: City Profile — score, cost, and nomad count in one view above the fold
Job tab on City — 20 open remote roles in Prague, without leaving the city page
Event tab on City — upcoming IRL meetups with attendee count visible
Feed tab — live activity from nomads currently in the city
One bookmark action across jobs and events — building a Prague shortlist, not three lists

data.text: The three apps aren't the problem. They're the symptom. Jobs, city research, and community discovery are organised by task type — when for a nomad, they're all the same question: 'Should I move to Prague?'
data.attribution: Drift design hypothesis — organise around the city, not the task

data.title: Each screen bets on what matters first.

items.title: Score first, description later.
items.body: Score (9.2) and Cost ($3,200/mo) appear before any city description text. The hypothesis: nomads make city decisions quantitatively first, qualitatively second. The description is confirmation, not discovery. The tab bar (Event 3 · Job 20) makes the city feel active — not a static wiki entry.

items.title: Four currencies. One surface.
items.body: Payment options (USD · Euro · BTC · ETH) appear as equal pill badges on the card — not inside the detail view. For a freelancer whose income is already in crypto, this is a qualifying signal that needs to be visible before the click. Opening 15 listings to find one that pays in ETH is friction that compounds.

items.title: Who's going before what it is.
items.body: 'Nomad joined' (99+ avatars) appears before 'About the event'. The decision to attend an IRL event is social before it's informational. Seeing your crowd there answers the real question — 'will I know anyone?' — faster than any event description can.

data.headline: What the layout is arguing — and what it gave up.

items.title: Payment options on the card, not the detail
items.description:
**Problem →** A crypto-earning freelancer treats payment type as a qualifying criterion — not a detail. Burying BTC/ETH inside the job detail means opening 15 listings to find the one that pays in the right currency.

**A:** Payment shown in detail view only — standard card pattern, preserves readability. **B:** Currency pills on the card surface.

**Chose B** — card-level visibility lets users filter by scanning, not clicking. **Cost:** Four pills consume space that could show two more lines of role description. The bet is that payment type matters more to this user than extra requirements text.

items.title: Social proof above event description
items.description:
**Problem →** Nomads don't primarily attend events for the content — they attend to meet people. A well-described event with zero nomads is less compelling than a vague one with 99 already in.

**A:** Standard layout — event details first, attendee list at the bottom. **B:** 'Nomad joined' count appears high on the page, above the description.

**Chose B** — the social decision happens before the informational one. **Cost:** A newcomer unfamiliar with the event concept has to scroll past the social proof to understand what they're showing up to.

items.title: City as a live content hub, not a static profile
items.description:
**Problem →** Events and jobs aren't useful in the abstract — they're useful because they're in Prague. Keeping them on separate platforms strips the location context that makes them relevant.

**A:** City page shows static info only — score, cost, description, map. **B:** City page becomes a hub with tabs for dynamic content: Feed · Events · Jobs.

**Chose B** — the city is the organising principle, not the task type. **Cost:** Tabs require users to discover they exist. Count badges (Event 3 · Job 20) do critical signalling work — without them the tabs read as empty navigation.

items.title: One bookmark action across jobs and events
items.description:
**Problem →** A nomad building a Prague shortlist wants to save a job and an event — but these live in different apps with different save actions. The intent is the same; the surfaces are fragmented.

**A:** Type-specific actions — RSVP for events, Save for jobs, different UI per content type. **B:** Single bookmark icon, identical across both screens.

**Chose B** — unified save intent produces a coherent Prague shortlist. **Cost:** On the event screen, bookmark now competes with 'Interest to join'. Two save-like actions create a mild hierarchy problem that was noted but not resolved in this sprint.

data.title: How the three screens connect
data.description: A nomad's relocation decision is a single session — not three separate app sessions. Drift's screens are designed as a sequential funnel: qualify the city, find the work, confirm the community. Each screen earns the right to the next: a livability score above threshold is the permission to dig into jobs, and job depth is the permission to check whether the social scene already exists.
steps: Open a job listing — verify crypto payment (ETH accepted)
Return to city — check Event tab
See 'Run For Hal Prague' — 99+ nomads joined

items.name: The crypto-native nomad
items.type: Composite · based on app's evident target user
items.biography: Earns in USDC or ETH. Moves cities every two to three months — not backpacking, but not settling either. Evaluates relocations the way some people evaluate apartments: score first, vibe second. Finds community through Bitcoin meetups and Telegram nomad groups rather than LinkedIn or Eventbrite.
items.goal: Find a city with a livable cost-to-income ratio, an active crypto-friendly remote job market, and enough nomads already there that the first two weeks don't feel like starting from zero.
items.needs: Quantitative city signals up front — not buried under a city description. Crypto payment options visible before clicking into a job listing. Event social proof (who's going) visible before the event description (what it is).
items.frustrations: Opening 15 job listings to find the one that pays in crypto. City research spread across four apps that don't share context. Showing up to a new city and discovering the nomad scene existed two weeks ago — and the next meetup is in a month.
items.why_use: Opens City Profile → checks Score and Cost before reading a word of the description. Scans Job 20 count on the tab to gauge market depth before opening any listings. Checks Event 3 tab to see what's happening in the next two weeks. The numbers on the tabs are the entire first pass — text comes second. If the numbers pass threshold, Prague is shortlisted in under 90 seconds. If they don't, the city is skipped without opening a single listing.

data.headline: What this sprint explored — and what it didn't.
data.body: Drift is a concept, not a shipped product. The sprint explored one hypothesis: organising city discovery, jobs, and community around a place — rather than a task type — forces specific decisions about information hierarchy and social proof ordering. The payment-type-on-card, social-proof-above-description, and count-badge-on-tab decisions all follow from that one reframing. What the concept does not validate: whether the crypto-native nomad audience is large enough to build for, whether a tab-heavy city hub creates navigation debt, or whether a unified bookmark across content types produces useful shortlists. Those remain open questions that would require user testing to resolve.

data.text: Nomads qualify cities with signals, not stories. Score before description. Social proof before event details. The ordering isn't aesthetic — it's the entire design argument, visible in three screens.
